title: Roast pork belly
subtitle: Three hours low, ten minutes high for the crackling
excerpt: Pork belly slow-roasted at low heat until tender and yielding, finished with a blast at maximum heat to blister the rind. The two-stage method is reliable: the low phase renders all the fat, and the final blast crisps what has already dried out. Apple sauce is the essential accompaniment.
sourceNotes: Pork belly was long considered the working-class cut in Britain — fatty, cheap, and overlooked for Sunday tables. Over the past two decades it moved to the centre of British restaurant cooking, and the home version followed. The two-stage method (low then blast) produces crackling and tender meat reliably, which the single-temperature methods do not.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Salt the rind the night before and leave uncovered in the fridge.
recipe.temperatureNote: 140°C fan for slow phase, then 240°C / 220°C fan for the blast

content.text: Score the rind in lines 1 cm apart, cutting through to the fat but not into the meat. Salt generously the night before and leave uncovered in the fridge — the rind should be visibly dry and white before it goes in the oven.

The low phase renders the fat and cooks the meat through without forcing anything. By the time you apply the blast, the rind is already dry and the fat beneath is liquid — it takes almost no time to blister. Watch the blast phase constantly; 10 minutes can become burnt crackling in under a minute past the window.

items.prepNote: rubbed into the rind and all surfaces
rubbed into the flesh side only
halved, to sit under the belly

content.text: Heat the oven to 160°C. Pat the rind of the {{pork-belly}} completely dry. Place rind-side up in a roasting tin. Scatter {{garlic}} under the belly if using. Season the flesh side with {{black-pepper}} and {{fennel-seeds}} if using. Roast at 160°C for 3 hours, until the meat is completely tender when prodded. Remove from the oven. Increase the temperature to 240°C and let it fully preheat. Return the belly to the oven and blast for 10 to 15 minutes, until the rind blisters and crackles. Watch it closely throughout — it can burn in under a minute. Remove and rest for 10 minutes. Cut through the rind with a sharp knife or kitchen scissors.

items.symptom: Crackling blistered but still slightly soft in places
items.cause: Uneven scoring or uneven rind thickness
items.fix: Normal variation in belly thickness means the thicker parts blister last. Give it another 3 to 5 minutes at full heat, or finish under the grill

items.symptom: Meat falling apart, difficult to portion
items.fix: Not a problem for flavour — just cut it in the tin rather than trying to lift it. Three hours at 160°C is a safe time; going to 3.5 hours is the upper limit before it becomes unportionable

items.cause: Low phase temperature too high or time too short
items.fix: The fat must fully render during the long low phase. If it is still thick after 3 hours, continue at 160°C until it softens, then apply the blast

content.text: Pork belly is the most fat-generous cut on the pig, and the slow-roast method is designed around that fat. The low phase converts the fat from solid to rendered without drying the meat, and the blast creates texture from what is left. It is a weekend lunch dish rather than a special occasion roast — the cut is inexpensive and the method is forgiving enough to leave in the oven unattended for most of the cook.
